standard.title: Classification and Biodiversity
standard.description: Investigate the role of classification in ordering and organising the diversity of life on Earth and use and develop classification tools including dichotomous keys
standard.prior_knowledge: Students can name and describe familiar living things and sort them into broad groups (animals, plants). They have not studied formal taxonomy or constructed classification tools.
standard.y_goal: Students explain why classification systems are used, apply and construct dichotomous keys, and evaluate how classification tools order and organise biodiversity.

nodes.label: Purpose of classification
nodes.xmin: State at least two reasons why scientists classify living things; give one example of classification in everyday life
nodes.width_core: Compare two different classification systems; explain which is more useful for a given purpose and why
width_enrich_options: Investigate how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples classify living things on Country and compare with Linnaean taxonomy — what does each system prioritise?
Research how classification systems have changed over time through improvements in microscopy; explain what this reveals about scientific knowledge
Consider how classification supports identification and communication in a real scientific or practical context (e.g. biosecurity, medicine)
nodes.y_description: Students can explain why classification is useful for organising and communicating about living things
success_criteria: Names at least two reasons why classification is useful
Gives a correct everyday example of classification
Distinguishes between classification for identification vs communication

nodes.label: Taxonomy: major groups and kingdoms
nodes.xmin: Name the major kingdoms; correctly place 5 familiar organisms into their kingdom with justification using observable features
nodes.width_core: Sort a set of 12 organisms into kingdoms; identify one organism that is difficult to classify and explain why
width_enrich_options: Name and classify species using Linnaean hierarchy (kingdom → species); explain what each level represents
Research a boundary case (e.g. Euglena, virus) — does it fit the kingdom model? What does this reveal about classification systems?
Observe and identify similarities and differences of particular features within and between groups of organisms using provided specimens or images
nodes.y_description: Students can place organisms into major taxonomic groups using observable features and justify their classification
success_criteria: Correctly places 5 familiar organisms into kingdoms with observable feature justification
Identifies at least one organism that is difficult to classify and explains why

nodes.label: Using dichotomous keys
nodes.xmin: Use a provided dichotomous key to correctly identify at least 5 organisms; explain how each decision point works
nodes.width_core: Use two different keys for the same organism set; identify where they agree and disagree and explain why
width_enrich_options: Use a provided dichotomous key to identify organisms surveyed on a field trip or from local environment
Evaluate a flawed dichotomous key — identify the error, explain its consequence, and propose a correction
Compare a scientific dichotomous key with a First Nations classification system for the same organisms — what features does each prioritise?
nodes.hinge_reason: Using a key requires students to apply binary logic to observable features — a prerequisite for constructing keys. Misconceptions about feature selection cluster here and propagate directly into Node 4.
nodes.y_description: Students can use a dichotomous key to identify organisms by applying binary decisions based on observable features
success_criteria: Follows all decision points in a provided key to reach correct identifications
Names the observable feature used at each decision point
Explains why an incorrect path leads to a wrong outcome

nodes.label: Constructing dichotomous keys
nodes.xmin: Construct a simple dichotomous key for a set of 6 organisms using observable features; test it with a peer
nodes.width_core: Construct and refine a key for 10 organisms; document one revision made after peer testing and explain the improvement
width_enrich_options: Create and modify a dichotomous key to classify organisms into groups and subgroups; evaluate its effectiveness
Design a key for a novel organism set (e.g. everyday objects or invented creatures); reflect on what makes a feature scientifically useful
Construct a key based on First Nations classification criteria for local organisms and compare its structure with a Linnaean key
nodes.hinge_reason: Construction requires students to reverse-engineer the logic of classification — the deepest understanding of the standard. Assessment items directly test this. Errors here are not recoverable without significant reteaching.
nodes.y_description: Students can construct a dichotomous key that correctly classifies a set of organisms using observable features
success_criteria: Constructs a key that correctly classifies all organisms in a given set
Key uses binary observable features at each decision point
Peer testing confirms key produces correct identifications

nodes.label: Classification systems change over time
nodes.xmin: Give one example of a reclassification event; explain what new evidence caused the change
nodes.width_core: Trace the reclassification of one organism — what evidence existed before, what changed, and how did the scientific community respond?
width_enrich_options: Examine how biological classification has changed through improvements in microscopy and DNA analysis
Evaluate: is reclassification a sign that science is unreliable, or a sign that it works? Construct an evidence-based argument
Investigate how First Nations classification knowledge has contributed to or challenged scientific classification in specific cases
nodes.y_description: Students can explain how classification systems change in response to new evidence and evaluate what this reveals about scientific knowledge
success_criteria: Names a real reclassification event and the evidence that caused it
Explains whether reclassification weakens or strengthens confidence in science
